Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I felt like it or until something was due, which meant some weeks I'd barely touch anything and other weeks I'd cram. I have about 14 hours a week that I dedicate to studying, but it's rarely at consistent times. I'd often study late at night when my apartment was quiet, reading through materials and making notes in my own notebooks rather than following any structured schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and whatever else was going on in my life. I'm involved in some extracurricular activities, so those would sometimes take priority over studying. When exams approached or assignments were due, I'd suddenly become much more focused, but during quieter periods, I'd drift and do the bare minimum. It wasn't intentional neglect—I just responded to pressure rather than planning ahead.
Q3: Usually a notification or remembering that something was due soon would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new posted, scroll through quickly, and then leave without doing much. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in a bit more because they felt less tedious than regular assignments. But honestly, most of my logins were reactive rather than proactive.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never quite became a habit for me—it always felt like something I had to force myself to do. There were a couple of weeks where I tried to make it routine, logging in every morning with my coffee, but that faded quickly. The gamified elements helped a bit because they made it feel less like a chore, but I never reached the point where it felt natural.
Q5: I found the reading materials and downloadable PDFs most useful because I could take them offline and work through them at my own pace. I'm a read-write learner, so having text I could highlight and annotate was valuable. The video lectures were okay too—I watched quite a bit of them—but I preferred having something I could physically mark up. Discussion posts occasionally had useful insights from classmates, though I didn't participate much myself.
Q6: I mostly ignored the peer interaction features and a lot of the optional discussion activities. They felt time-consuming for unclear payoff, and I'm not great at engaging with strangers online. I also delayed a lot of the smaller assignments, which explains my low completion rate. If something didn't seem immediately necessary for passing, I'd push it aside and often forget about it entirely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exams or final grades. I also appreciated when something was genuinely interesting or when the gamified elements made it feel like less of a slog. If I could see clear progress or earn something tangible, I was more likely to engage. Vague or open-ended tasks without clear outcomes felt like a waste of my limited energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, while required ones felt like obligations I had to eventually deal with. I rarely did optional work unless it was gamified or seemed genuinely interesting. Required tasks got done—eventually—but often at the last possible moment. There's something about the lack of pressure with optional work that made it easy to dismiss.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything I could and studied from those files offline. I'd print readings, make handwritten notes, and use my own textbooks and external resources to supplement the course material. A lot of my actual learning happened away from my laptop, sitting at my desk with physical materials spread out. The LMS was more of a delivery system than my actual study environment.
Q10: There were many times when I was actively studying but the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours reviewing printed notes, re-reading downloaded PDFs, or working through practice problems in a notebook. Sometimes I'd watch a video once, take detailed notes, and then review those notes repeatedly without ever logging back in. My exam scores reflect learning that definitely didn't show up in my activity logs.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd download a PDF or watch a video once, then work with that material offline for days. I preferred having everything saved locally so I didn't need internet access or to deal with the LMS interface. Once I had what I needed, I rarely went back to the platform unless I had to submit something or check for updates.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I just couldn't bring myself to engage. Usually it was when I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars or when I was feeling anxious about other things. Sometimes the sheer volume of content across my 18 online courses felt overwhelming, and I'd shut down rather than try to tackle it. My resilience usually kicked in eventually, but there were definite low points.
Q13: Workload from other courses and competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. When I was busy with extracurriculars or had multiple deadlines across different courses, this one would slip down my priority list. Fatigue played a role too—after a long day, the last thing I wanted was to log into another platform. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and sometimes tomorrow never came.
Q14: Oh, definitely. There were times I'd log in, open a video or document, and just stare at it without really absorbing anything. My mind would wander, or I'd be too tired to actually process the information. I'd technically be 'active' on the platform but getting nothing out of it. Those sessions always felt like wasted time, but I'd count them as studying anyway.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even important ones. I think it's partly anxiety—the bigger something feels, the more I avoid starting it. I'd convince myself I worked better under pressure, which was sometimes true but often just an excuse. The assignments I completed were usually done in a last-minute rush, which probably explains my mediocre completion rate despite decent exam scores.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift and procrastinate indefinitely. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due, then drop to almost nothing afterward. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of deadlines was my primary motivator throughout the course.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback felt good but didn't necessarily change my behavior. Critical feedback sometimes motivated me to do better, but other times it made me want to avoid that type of task entirely. The gamified feedback—points, badges, progress indicators—actually worked better for me than written comments because it felt less personal and more like a game.
Q18: The course structure gave me a lot of flexibility, which was both good and bad. Good because I could work at my own pace when motivated; bad because I could easily fall behind when I wasn't. Having 18 online courses meant I was constantly juggling, and this course's structure didn't force me to stay on track. I needed more external structure than was provided.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier—they broke things into smaller, more manageable pieces and gave me immediate feedback. What made it harder was the sheer amount of content and the lack of clear prioritization. I often couldn't tell what was essential versus what was supplementary, so I'd either try to do everything or give up and do almost nothing.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure things out or searching for what they need without actually producing output. Maybe they're anxious about submitting imperfect work, or they're exploring without committing. It could also be that they're using the LMS as a reference while doing work elsewhere. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I performed reasonably well on exams despite low LMS activity because I was learning offline. I'd download materials, study from printed notes, and use external resources. The LMS activity doesn't capture the hours I spent with a highlighter and my notebooks. Some students just don't need to be constantly logged in to learn effectively.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and whether you submitted things. But they can't show whether you actually understood the material, how much time you spent thinking about it offline, or whether you were genuinely engaged versus just going through the motions. They capture behavior but not learning. They miss the quality of engagement entirely.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about and thinking deeply about the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, when I'm connecting ideas, when I'm motivated to understand rather than just pass. Engagement isn't about how many times I log in—it's about whether my mind is actually present and working when I'm studying. Sometimes my best engagement happens far from any platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually learned from downloaded materials, the connections I made while reviewing my handwritten notes, or the genuine interest I developed in certain topics. It wouldn't capture my late-night study sessions with printed readings or the conversations I had with friends about course concepts. My engagement was real—it just happened mostly offline.
